title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who volunteered at a community theatre festival. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

texts.A:
### Marta, The Props Coordinator
Marta helped with props for the community theatre festival after lending an old suitcase to one production and being unexpectedly invited back for the whole week. She expected the job to involve finding pretty objects, but soon learned that actors needed items strong enough to survive repeated use. [47]Her experience repairing furniture at home helped her strengthen a wobbly chair that had to collapse convincingly without actually injuring anyone.[/47] She labelled every prop table with photographs because performers often returned things in a panic. [48]The most stressful moment was discovering, ten minutes before curtain-up, that a fake letter central to the plot had been thrown away with real rubbish.[/48] Marta rewrote it by hand while an actor improvised backstage. She now keeps duplicate copies of anything made from paper or cardboard. She says the audience rarely notices props unless something goes wrong, which is exactly why she enjoys the work.
texts.B:
### Owen, The Ticket Desk Volunteer
Owen joined the festival ticket desk because he wanted a calm role that involved sitting down. That hope lasted about one evening. [49]He quickly discovered that the hardest part was explaining politely why latecomers could not enter during the opening scene, even when they had driven across town.[/49] He built a simple colour-coded seating chart to prevent arguments about reserved front rows. [50]His background as a hotel receptionist helped him stay cheerful with confused visitors who had booked for the wrong night or venue.[/50] Owen enjoyed recognising regular audience members and learning which seats they preferred during comedies. He was surprised by how much the front desk quietly shaped people’s mood before the show began. A warm greeting could rescue a rainy evening; a careless one could make everything feel disorganised. He now arrives early to check signs, pens and change before anyone else notices they are missing.
texts.C:
### Jo, The Youth Cast Assistant
Jo volunteered with the youth cast after her younger brother unexpectedly joined the festival’s first teenage production. [51]She had initially worried that she would be treated as an annoying older sister rather than a useful assistant.[/51] Instead, the cast asked her to run quick warm-up games and help them remember entrances. Jo’s recent drama school audition experience was unexpectedly useful when nervous teenagers wanted advice on breathing and posture. [52]She was moved when a shy actor who had barely spoken in rehearsals delivered the difficult final speech clearly on opening night.[/52] The work was noisy, and keeping everyone focused between long scenes required more energy than performing. Gradually, Jo learned not to solve every problem herself; sometimes the young actors needed time to negotiate. She hopes the festival will keep giving teenagers difficult material, not just cheerful background parts, because they clearly proved they could handle it.
texts.D:
### Ravi, The Lighting Volunteer
Ravi joined the lighting team after the electrician at his workplace mentioned that the festival needed people who were comfortable with basic cables. [53]He was surprised by how much artistic judgement was involved in lighting, since a small change in brightness could alter the mood of a whole scene.[/53] At first he relied too heavily on the director’s pencilled notes and missed cues whenever actors paused unexpectedly. [54]His worst error was blacking out the stage a full page early during a dress rehearsal, leaving everyone frozen in silence.[/54] The cast forgave him, but he practised with the script for several hours afterwards. Ravi enjoyed the secret teamwork of technicians wearing headsets in the dark. [55]He now writes cue numbers in large print on coloured cards so substitute operators can follow the show if needed.[/55] [56]He says he will remember the applause for the crew more than any individual compliment.[/56]

questions.question: used repair skills to make a stage object safe but believable?
questions.explanation: Marta used furniture repair experience to strengthen a chair that had to collapse safely.

questions.question: had to recreate an important paper item shortly before a performance began?
questions.explanation: Marta rewrote a fake letter ten minutes before curtain-up after it was thrown away.

questions.question: found it difficult to enforce an entry rule with disappointed audience members?
questions.explanation: Owen had to explain why latecomers could not enter during the opening scene.

questions.question: used hospitality experience to deal calmly with booking mistakes?
questions.explanation: Owen’s hotel receptionist background helped him handle visitors who booked the wrong night.

questions.question: had worried that a family link would make others take them less seriously?
questions.explanation: Jo worried she would be seen as an annoying older sister rather than a useful assistant.

questions.question: was touched by a quiet performer’s success during the actual show?
questions.explanation: Jo was moved when a shy actor delivered the final speech clearly on opening night.

questions.question: was surprised that a technical role required creative decisions?
questions.explanation: Ravi realised small changes in brightness could alter a scene’s mood.

questions.question: made a timing mistake that left the performers unexpectedly still?
questions.explanation: Ravi blacked out the stage too early, leaving everyone frozen.

questions.question: prepares visual cue notes so someone else could operate the show?
questions.explanation: Ravi writes cue numbers on large coloured cards for substitute operators.

questions.question: values recognition given to the whole technical team more than personal praise?
questions.explanation: Ravi says he will remember applause for the crew more than any individual compliment.
